prompt: We need to reconstruct the authorization matrix from a COBOL mainframe application that was migrated to Java 15 years ago. The COBOL source is available but uncommented. The Java port diverged significantly. Which system's authorization model is the 'source of truth' and what are the gaps?

A startup we acquired had a recommendation engine. The ML model weights are in a pickle file, the training data is gone, and the feature engineering code references columns that don't exist in any surviving database schema. Reconstruct what this model was trying to predict and what signals it used.

Our old monitoring system (Nagios, circa 2014) had custom check scripts. The new system (Datadog) needs equivalent monitors. The Nagios configs are in a tarball but reference host groups and service templates that are deeply nested. Extract the actual monitoring logic and thresholds.

There's a dead Slack bot from 3 years ago whose code references an internal API that also no longer exists. People remember it could do expense approvals. I need to understand its approval workflow from the code alone — what were the approval thresholds, escalation rules, and notification patterns?

We have an archived Terraform state file and the corresponding HCL configs from a decommissioned AWS environment. Legal wants to know exactly what data stores existed, what encryption was configured, and whether any S3 buckets were publicly accessible at the time of decommission.

A former CTO left behind a design document repo with ADRs (Architecture Decision Records). Half of them reference other ADRs that were never written. Reconstruct the decision chain for their move from monolith to microservices, noting where the reasoning trail goes cold.

We have git blame data and Jira ticket references from a module that was rewritten three times. Each rewrite's commit messages blame the previous version. I need to separate actual technical debt from interpersonal friction in these records to understand what genuinely needed fixing.

An iOS app from 2017 used a custom binary protocol over WebSockets. The protocol spec was in a Google Doc that got deleted when the author's account was deprovisioned. Only the client-side Swift parsing code and a packet capture file remain. Reconstruct the wire protocol.
